figures.caption: Fig. 1: System model of FEDKAD for federated IoT multivariate time-series anomaly detection.
Fig. 2: Non-IID heterogeneity across the four benchmarks, covering feature distribution skew, label distribution skew, and quantity skew.
TABLE IV: FedKAD robustness under supplementary evaluation protocols. The best baseline is selected per dataset and protocol.

Fig. 4: Federated efficiency: training wall-clock (s), per-round communication (KB) and inference latency (µs/step), log scale. FEDKAD is two to three orders of magnitude faster to train and one to two orders cheaper in communication than the next-best neural baseline, with comparable or lower inference latency.

Fig. 5: Empirical convergence diagnostics on SMD (N=28 clients, r=24, 30 rounds). (A) Mean consensus residual 1 N ∑ i ∥P ℓ i −Zℓ∥F : decays at ρ≥1 (Theorem V.5). (B) Augmented Lagrangian Lℓ: descends toward a bounded neighbourhood at ρ=1; the late-round oscillation is consistent with Theorem V.3 under partial client participation and the ∆ℓ Q perturbation from the Qi-refit (A5). (C) Local objective ∑ i f ℓ i (P ℓ i ): decreases over training.

Fig. 6: On-device benchmark on a Raspberry Pi 4 under the SMAP setting. FEDKAD achieves the best edge-deployment trade-off, requiring only 59 KB upload per round while reducing training and inference time by up to 25× and 31× over the slowest baseline.
